threats.title: Router._validateDecimals uint8 Underflow Blocks Valid Pool Creation
threats.hypothesis_description: The _validateDecimals function performs unsigned subtraction `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` which reverts on underflow in Solidity 0.8+ when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals. This silently blocks creation of all valid pools where the base token has more decimals than the quote token through the Router (createPool, createPoolETH).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic code bug affecting core pool creation functionality. Any token pair where base decimals exceed quote decimals (e.g., 18-decimal base with 6-decimal quote) cannot be created through the Router. The decimals difference might be well within the acceptable range but the unidirectional check blocks it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint8 subtraction `quoteDecimals - baseDecimals` underflows when baseDecimals > quoteDecimals in Solidity 0.8+

preconditions: User attempts to create a pool via Router where base token has more decimals than quote token
Decimal difference is within acceptable range (e.g., 18 vs 6 = 12, within MAX_BASE_QUOTE_DECIMALS_DIFFERENCE)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: quoteDecimals - baseDecimals with uint8 types underflows instead of computing absolute difference
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack per se, but breaks core protocol functionality for common token pairs
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Prevents legitimate pool creation, forcing users to use Factory.create directly (bypassing Router safety checks)

economic_questions: What proportion of common token pairs have baseDecimals > quoteDecimals?
Are there deployed pools affected by this restriction?
Does bypassing Router via direct Factory.create expose users to other risks?
validation_requirements: Test createPool with a base token of 18 decimals and quote token of 6 decimals
Confirm revert with arithmetic underflow panic rather than ErrDecimalsDifferenceTooLarge
Verify Factory.create works directly for the same pair

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - code defect, not capital-based attack
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: forces users to bypass Router safety, potential loss from missing slippage protection

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Blocks creation of common token pair pools (e.g., any 18-decimal base with 6-decimal quote)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users attempting to create pools with base decimals > quote decimals via Router
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete inability to create desired pool through Router
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All liquidity for affected token pairs

investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - any affected pool creation attempt fails

threats.title: MagicLP Flash Loan Fee Bypass When Both Token Balances Maintained
threats.hypothesis_description: In MagicLP.flashLoan, if the borrower returns both base and quote tokens such that both post-callback balances are >= their respective reserves, neither the sell_quote nor sell_base validation branch executes. The flash loan completes with zero fees charged. Only _sync() is called, updating reserves to the new (equal or higher) balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The flashLoan function's fee logic is conditional on detecting a balance decrease in at least one token. An attacker who borrows tokens, uses them profitably externally, and returns them (or equivalent value in both tokens) pays zero protocol fees. This constitutes lost revenue for LPs and the maintainer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Two separate `if` checks for balance < reserve; if neither triggers, no fee path executes. Only condition checked is `both < reserve` which reverts.

preconditions: Flash loan available from MagicLP pool with sufficient liquidity
Attacker can use borrowed tokens profitably in external protocols within same transaction
Attacker returns both base and quote tokens >= their pre-loan reserves

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: flashLoan lines 263-292: sell_quote branch requires baseBalance < _BASE_RESERVE_, sell_base branch requires quoteBalance < _QUOTE_RESERVE_. Neither triggers when both are >=.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Borrow tokens → use in external arbitrage/governance/oracle manipulation → return tokens with no fee
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 100% of external arbitrage profit captured since zero flash loan fee. LP fee revenue = 0 for these operations.

economic_questions: How much arbitrage volume could exploit free flash loans?
What is the revenue loss to LPs and maintainer per free flash loan?
Can the free flash loan be used to manipulate prices in other protocols?
validation_requirements: Execute flashLoan with data callback that returns exact borrowed amounts
Confirm no revert and no fee charged
Verify _sync updates reserves to pre-loan values

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on external arbitrage opportunity; 100% captured due to zero fee
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite ROI on zero capital (minus gas)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool must have sufficient liquidity for desired borrow
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol fee revenue lost; no direct LP fund loss

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Depends on flash loan volume; cumulative fee revenue loss
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All potential flash loan fee revenue

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify both-balance-maintained path skips fee logic entirely
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace code path when baseBalance >= BASE_RESERVE AND quoteBalance >= QUOTE_RESERVE
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: medium - no on-chain fee event emitted for free flash loans

threats.title: sellShares Target Reduction Asymmetric Rounding Causes Cumulative Degradation
threats.hypothesis_description: In sellShares, _BASE_TARGET_ and _QUOTE_TARGET_ are reduced using divCeil (rounding up the subtraction), while baseAmount and quoteAmount given to users use floor division (rounding down). This asymmetric rounding means targets shrink faster than reserves proportionally. Over many sellShares operations, this cumulative degradation distorts PMM pricing and could eventually cause targets to reach zero while reserves remain positive.
threats.investigation_rationale: The divCeil vs floor division asymmetry is a clear code defect in the accounting. Each operation loses up to 1 wei of target precision. While individually insignificant, in pools with high transaction volume this compounds, gradually worsening PMM curve accuracy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: baseAmount uses floor division (line 332) while _BASE_TARGET_ uses divCeil (line 335). quoteAmount uses floor (line 333) while _QUOTE_TARGET_ uses divCeil (line 336).

preconditions: Pool with active LP share trading
Repeated sellShares operations accumulating rounding errors

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Target: TARGET - divCeil(TARGET * shareAmount, totalShares). Reserve: floor(balance * shareAmount / totalShares). Each op loses up to 1 wei per target.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Many small sellShares operations maximize rounding error accumulation rate
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker could intentionally perform many small sellShares to degrade targets, then exploit distorted PMM pricing

economic_questions: How many operations needed to materially degrade pricing?
Can an attacker profitably exploit the degraded pricing curve?
At what point do targets reach zero causing PMM failure?
validation_requirements: Simulate 10000+ sellShares operations and measure target drift
Calculate the pricing error as a percentage of trade value
Test PMM behavior when targets approach zero

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: LP tokens for repeated sell operations
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Negligible per operation, cumulative over time
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very low - rounding error is 1 wei per operation
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Requires extremely many operations for material impact

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Any pool with LP share trading

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Minimal per operation, cumulative over pool lifetime
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Pool pricing accuracy, potential pricing failure at extreme degradation

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: divCeil error = 1 wei max per target per operation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare floor(balance * share / total) vs ceil(target * share / total) over many iterations
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: low per operation, high for material exploitation

threats.title: LockingMultiRewards Operator-Only Lock Processing Freezes User Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks is gated by onlyOperators with no fallback mechanism for users to unlock their own expired positions. If the operator (Gelato automation) goes offline, loses credentials, or experiences extended downtime, all users with locked tokens cannot access their funds regardless of lock expiration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The protocol documentation confirms reliance on Gelato for lock processing. There is no user-callable function to process their own expired locks. This creates a single point of failure where operational issues completely freeze user funds.
threats.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol shows deployment with lock processing depending on external Gelato automation. No emergency unlock function exists.

proof_of_signal.pattern: processExpiredLocks has onlyOperators modifier (line 397). No alternative user-callable unlock function exists in the contract.

preconditions: Users have locked staking tokens
Lock period has expired
Operator (Gelato) is offline or unable to process transactions

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Lock expiry: unlockTime <= block.timestamp, but no user-accessible function checks this
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Single point of failure in operator dependency
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but systemic risk of fund freezing. Malicious operator could selectively refuse to process specific users.

economic_questions: What is the total value locked that depends on operator processing?
What is the Gelato uptime SLA and failure history?
Could owner add a new operator as emergency fallback?
validation_requirements: Verify no user-callable unlock function exists
Confirm withdraw() only works for unlocked balance, not expired locks
Test that pausing doesn't help users access expired locks

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - operational risk, not capital-based attack
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A for attacker; total locked TVL at risk for users

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All locked positions frozen, potential protocol trust crisis

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with expired lock positions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Search entire contract for alternative unlock paths
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - users notice immediately when locks don't process

threats.title: Reward Token Transfer Failure Permanently Bricks All User Reward Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: If any reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, or reverts on transfer), the _getRewards function permanently reverts for all affected users. Since reward tokens cannot be removed from the rewardTokens array, and _getRewards iterates all tokens in a single transaction, failure of one token blocks claiming of ALL reward tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _getRewards function loops through all rewardTokens and performs safeTransfer for each expired lock item. If any single token reverts, the entire transaction fails. There's no mechanism to skip individual tokens or remove problematic ones from the array.
deployment_assumptions: One or more reward tokens could become non-transferable (pause, blacklist, or upgrade)
threats.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol adds ARB as reward token. If ARB or any future reward token implements pause/blacklist functionality, this risk materializes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getRewards iterates all rewardTokens in single tx. safeTransfer at line 483 reverts if token is non-transferable. No try-catch, no skip mechanism, no removeReward function.

preconditions: At least one reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, upgraded)
Users have non-zero accumulated reward amounts for the failed token
The reward lock for the user has expired (triggering safeTransfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rewardTokens array is append-only (no removal). _getRewards loops all tokens atomically.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: External token governance action (pause/blacklist) creates cascading failure
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but all user rewards across ALL tokens become permanently frozen

economic_questions: Do any configured reward tokens have pause/blacklist capability?
What is the total accumulated reward value at risk?
Can users still withdraw staking tokens without claiming rewards?
validation_requirements: Verify withdraw() doesn't call _getRewards (it doesn't - separate path)
Confirm no removeReward function exists
Test _getRewards behavior when one reward token reverts on transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - depends on external token governance
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A for attacker; all accumulated rewards at risk

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete reward claiming failure across all tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All stakers with accumulated rewards for the problematic token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of all accumulated rewards frozen (not just the failed token)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending and locked rewards across all reward tokens

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace _getRewards when safeTransfer reverts at any index
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: depends on external token - outside attacker control

threats.title: notifyRewardAmount Near Epoch Boundary With Zero TotalSupply Permanently Loses Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: When notifyRewardAmount is called with very small _remainingRewardTime (near epoch boundary) and minRemainingTime=0, the rewardRate becomes extremely high but is only distributed for a tiny time window. If totalSupply is 0 during this window (no stakers), all rewards are permanently unclaimable as rewardPerToken doesn't increase.
threats.investigation_rationale: The combination of high rewardRate concentrated into a short window and zero totalSupply creates a permanent reward loss. The reward tokens remain in the contract but can never be distributed. The minRemainingTime parameter exists as a safety check but is optional (can be set to 0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: rewardRate = amount / _remainingRewardTime (line 380). If _remainingRewardTime=1, rate=amount/1. _rewardPerToken returns stored value unchanged when totalSupply_=0 (line 279).

preconditions: Operator calls notifyRewardAmount with minRemainingTime=0 near epoch boundary
totalSupply is 0 (no stakers) during the remaining reward window
Or all stakers withdraw before reward distribution completes

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rewardRate = 1000e18 / 1 = 1000e18/sec. With totalSupply=0, pendingRewardsPerToken = 0. All 1000e18 tokens stranded.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator error or malicious timing of reward notification
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Reward tokens permanently locked in contract. If recover() is called for reward token, owner gets them instead of stakers.

economic_questions: What is the typical totalSupply during epoch transitions?
Can an attacker withdraw all stake just before epoch boundary to trigger zero supply?
How much reward value could be stranded per occurrence?
validation_requirements: Test notifyRewardAmount with _remainingRewardTime = 1 and totalSupply = 0
Verify rewards are permanently unclaimable after periodFinish
Check if remaining rewards carry over to next notifyRewardAmount call

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Enough staking tokens to be sole staker, then withdraw all
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Griefing: entire reward notification amount lost
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - griefing attack, attacker doesn't directly profit

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker must be sole/majority staker to reduce totalSupply to 0
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Right before epoch boundary when operator is about to notify
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Full reward amount for the period permanently lost

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All future stakers who would have earned those rewards
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of lost reward amount

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: rewardsDuration=7 days, minimum _remainingRewardTime check is optional
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: medium - requires coordination with operator timing
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - reward tokens visible in contract balance but never distributed

threats.title: processExpiredLocks lastLockIndex Restriction Delays Fund Unlocking
threats.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks explicitly prevents processing the lock at lastLockIndex[user] when locks.length > 1, forcing operators to process locks in a specific order. If a user's most recently created lock expires before older locks (due to epoch alignment), it cannot be processed until all other locks are handled first, even if those haven't expired yet.
threats.investigation_rationale: The check `if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex` creates a mandatory processing order. Combined with the epoch-aligned unlock times, this can create scenarios where a user's expired lock cannot be processed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex. Most recent lock cannot be processed unless it's the only one.

preconditions: User has multiple locks (locks.length > 1)
Lock at lastLockIndex has expired
Other locks have not expired
Operator attempts to process the expired lastLockIndex lock

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: lastLockIndex is tracked separately from lock order. processExpiredLocks uses swap-and-pop which can reorder locks.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After swap-and-pop reorders locks, lastLockIndex may point to a middle element. Processing order constraints prevent optimal unlock timing.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User's expired funds remain locked until other locks also expire. No direct profit for attacker but griefing potential.

economic_questions: How frequently does the lock ordering constraint cause delays?
What is the maximum time a user's expired funds could be delayed?
Can the operator work around this by processing locks in specific order?
validation_requirements: Create a user with multiple locks where lastLockIndex lock expires first
Attempt to process it and confirm revert
Determine the delay until it can be processed

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - design constraint, not capital attack
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Up to one lockDuration (13 weeks) of delay

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When user has multiple locks with different expiry times
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Funds frozen beyond intended lock period

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users with multiple locks where most recent expires first
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lock amount frozen beyond intended expiry

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: maxLocks = lockDuration / rewardsDuration, various lock timing scenarios
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace lock creation and processing order constraints
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: low - specific lock ordering triggers the issue
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: medium - manifests only with specific lock patterns

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Breaks LockingMultiRewards Staking Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: LockingMultiRewards._stakeFor transfers `amount` tokens via safeTransferFrom and then adds `amount` to stakingTokenBalance. If the staking token is fee-on-transfer, the actual received amount is less than `amount`, but stakingTokenBalance is inflated. Over time, stakingTokenBalance exceeds actual token balance, causing insolvency when later users try to withdraw.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract never checks the actual received amount (balance before/after transfer pattern). If deployed with a fee-on-transfer staking token, the accounting desynchronizes immediately. Last withdrawers will fail as the contract lacks sufficient tokens.
threats.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol deploys with MIM as staking token. MIM is standard ERC20 without transfer fees currently. Risk applies if contract is reused with fee-on-transfer tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, this, amount) followed by stakingTokenBalance += amount without checking actual received amount

preconditions: Staking token implements fee-on-transfer (deducts fee during transfer)
Users stake tokens, inflating stakingTokenBalance above actual balance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: stakingTokenBalance += amount, but actual received = amount - fee. Cumulative desync = sum(fees).
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Last withdrawers cannot withdraw as contract is insolvent
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not attacker-exploitable for profit directly, but causes user fund loss

economic_questions: Is the current staking token (MIM) fee-on-transfer? (No, currently)
Could the contract be reused or forked with fee-on-transfer tokens?
Does the same issue affect notifyRewardAmount for reward tokens?
validation_requirements: Test with fee-on-transfer mock token
Confirm stakingTokenBalance exceeds actual balance after staking
Verify last withdrawer reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - accounting bug, not direct attack

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: From first stake with fee-on-transfer token

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All stakers if staking token has transfer fee
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Last withdrawers lose their entire staked balance
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative transfer fees over all stake operations

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify MIM token does not have transfer fees
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check balance before/after pattern absence in _stakeFor

threats.title: tx.origin Usage in Fee Rate Determination Enables Fee Bypass via Intermediary Contracts
threats.hypothesis_description: sellBase and sellQuote pass tx.origin (not msg.sender) to querySellBase/querySellQuote for fee rate determination. If the FeeRateModel implementation differentiates fees by address, a privileged EOA (e.g., whitelisted for lower fees) could be used as tx.origin while an intermediary contract performs the actual trade, bypassing per-address fee controls.
threats.investigation_rationale: The use of tx.origin for fee determination means the identity of the actual trader (the contract calling sellBase/sellQuote) is invisible to the fee model. This breaks the intended per-caller fee differentiation and opens the door for fee manipulation.
deployment_assumptions: FeeRateModel implementation differentiates fees by trader address
threats.config_evidence: FeeRateModelImpl.sol ignores trader parameter entirely (returns same rate for all). Risk materializes only if implementation is changed to address-specific fees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: querySellBase(tx.origin, baseInput) uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for fee rate lookup

preconditions: FeeRateModel implementation differentiates fees by trader address
A privileged tx.origin (lower fee rate) is available
Intermediary contract routes trades through that tx.origin

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Fee rate = getFeeRate(tx.origin, lpFeeRate) not getFeeRate(msg.sender, lpFeeRate)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Route trades through whitelisted EOA to get reduced fees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Difference between normal fee rate and whitelisted fee rate * trade volume

economic_questions: Does the current FeeRateModel differentiate by address?
What fee savings are possible with tx.origin manipulation?
Could this be used to grief other traders by changing their effective fee rate?
validation_requirements: Deploy FeeRateModel with address-specific rates
Verify sellBase uses tx.origin for lookup
Confirm intermediary contract inherits EOA's fee rate

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduced fee revenue for LPs and maintainer

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Depends on fee differential and volume

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current FeeRateModelImpl ignores address - check if planned changes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify tx.origin vs msg.sender in all swap paths
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: high - looks like normal EOA trades

threats.title: recover() Function Lacks Reward Token Solvency Guard Enabling Owner to Drain Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: The recover() function in LockingMultiRewards only checks balance sufficiency when the recovered token is the staking token. For reward tokens, there is no check that the remaining balance covers pending rewards, locked reward amounts, and ongoing distribution. Owner can recover all reward tokens, making the contract insolvent for reward claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has explicit protection for the staking token (ErrSkimmingTooMuch check) but NO equivalent protection for reward tokens. This asymmetric protection is a clear code defect - the guard should extend to reward tokens based on earmarked amounts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only stakingToken has balance protection (line 325-327). Reward tokens can be fully drained: tokenAddress.safeTransfer(owner, tokenAmount) with no solvency check.

preconditions: Owner calls recover() with a reward token address
tokenAmount exceeds excess (non-earmarked) reward token balance
Users have pending/locked rewards in that token

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No calculation for earmarked reward amount: sum(rewardRate * remainingTime) + sum(rewards[user][token]) + sum(rewardLock.items[token])
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner calls recover(rewardToken, rewardToken.balanceOf(this)) to drain all
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Owner extracts full reward token balance; users cannot claim rewards

economic_questions: What is the total earmarked reward token balance at any time?
Would getRewards calls revert after recovery or silently transfer 0?
Can this happen accidentally with a well-intentioned owner?
validation_requirements: Call recover() with reward token for more than excess amount
Verify subsequent getRewards() reverts for affected users
Confirm no solvency check exists for reward tokens

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full reward token balance in contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When reward balance is highest (just after notifyRewardAmount)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All stakers with accumulated or locked rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total reward token balance held by contract

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare stakingToken guard logic vs absent reward token guard
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify recover has no rewardToken-specific check

threats.title: setParameters Allows Implementation Owner to Extract Arbitrary Pool Reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicLP.setParameters allows the implementation owner to specify arbitrary baseOutAmount and quoteOutAmount to be transferred to any assetTo address. Combined with _resetTargetAndReserve which resets targets and RState to match post-extraction balances, this enables complete drainage of pool reserves to any address in a single transaction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function is gated by onlyImplementationOwner and has minBaseReserve/minQuoteReserve checks, but these can be set to 0 by the caller. Since these are caller-supplied minimums (not protocol-enforced), a compromised or malicious admin can drain all LP funds.
threats.config_evidence: MIMSwap.s.sol shows implementation ownership transferred to safe.ops multisig. No timelock is configured.

proof_of_signal.pattern: baseOutAmount and quoteOutAmount are transferred to assetTo (line 378-379) with only caller-supplied minReserve checks (line 364-366). _resetTargetAndReserve normalizes state post-extraction.

preconditions: Attacker has implementation owner access (compromised key, malicious admin)
Pool has non-zero reserves

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setParameters(attacker, validFeeRate, validI, validK, ALL_BASE, ALL_QUOTE, 0, 0) drains everything
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Single privileged transaction extracts all pool reserves

economic_questions: What is the total TVL across all MagicLP pools controlled by this implementation?
Is the implementation owner behind a timelock or multisig?
Is there monitoring for setParameters calls?
validation_requirements: Verify setParameters can drain reserves to 0
Confirm _resetTargetAndReserve creates valid (but empty) state
Check no timelock is deployed

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Total pool TVL (all base + quote reserves)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of all LP funds

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All LPs in the affected pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: minBaseReserve and minQuoteReserve are caller-supplied, not enforced minimums
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace setParameters with maximal extraction amounts
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - dramatic balance change immediately visible
